title: How One Mom's 'Silly' Trivia Business Became a Community Empire
guestBio: Christina Wooldridge is the founder of Trivia Goat, a live entertainment company bringing tech-enabled trivia to bars, breweries, and restaurants. With a background in hotel/restaurant management and nonprofit fundraising, she's a working mom of two based in Orange County, CA who transformed her hospitality expertise into a thriving community-building business.
excerpt: What if your 'silly' business idea could outperform the serious ones? Christina Wooldridge turned a simple trivia concept into a thriving community empire while navigating motherhood and redefining what success really means.
metaDescription: Discover how Christina Wooldridge built Trivia Goat into a community-centered business while being a working mom. Hospitality meets entrepreneurship.
keyTakeaways: Hospitality isn't a soft skill—it's a leadership superpower that creates real power in any conversation.
Your business idea doesn't need to be prestigious to be valuable and profitable.
Human connection, not just products or services, is what keeps customers coming back long-term.
Scaling doesn't always mean doing more—sometimes it means packaging your expertise differently.
Motherhood sharpens leadership skills through forced intentionality and laser focus.
The real value in business comes from creating belonging and community, not just transactions.
Unconventional ideas often succeed because they solve real human needs that others overlook.

pullQuotes.text: Hospitality is not a soft skill — it's a leadership superpower
Your business idea doesn't have to be 'prestigious' to be valuable
The real thing that keeps customers coming back is human connection

timestamps.label: Christina's through line — from childhood parades to live events
Motherhood, identity, and finding value beyond productivity
What Trivia Goat is and how it works

content:
What if the business idea you've been dismissing as "silly" or "not serious enough" could actually become your most successful venture? That's exactly what happened to Christina Wooldridge, founder of Trivia Goat, who turned what seemed like a simple concept into a thriving community-building empire.

In this powerful conversation with Aimee Rickabus on The Manage Her Podcast, Christina shares how she transformed her hospitality background into a tech-enabled trivia business that's creating real connection in an increasingly disconnected world. But this isn't just a business success story—it's a masterclass in redefining value, embracing unconventional ideas, and building something meaningful while navigating the complexities of working motherhood.

The Thread That Connects Everything

Christina's entrepreneurial journey didn't start with a business plan—it started with childhood parades. As a young girl organizing neighborhood festivities, she was unknowingly developing the skills that would later become her superpower: bringing people together and creating experiences that matter. This thread of community-building wove through her hotel and restaurant management career, into nonprofit fundraising, and ultimately into founding Trivia Goat.

What's fascinating about Christina's path is how she recognized this pattern in her own life. Too often, we dismiss our natural inclinations as "not real work" or "just something we're good at." Christina shows us that these innate abilities—especially when they involve connecting people—can become the foundation of a profitable, meaningful business.

Motherhood as Leadership Training

One of the most striking insights from this conversation is how Christina views motherhood as leadership training rather than a career obstacle. When she became a mother, the usual quick fixes and band-aid solutions stopped working. Motherhood forced her to think long-term, to be intentional with her time, and to focus on what truly matters.

This shift in perspective transformed not just her parenting but her entire approach to business. The scattered, people-pleasing version of herself had to evolve into someone who could make clear decisions, set boundaries, and operate with laser focus. As Christina puts it, motherhood didn't diminish her professional capabilities—it sharpened them.

The revelation that her worth wasn't tied to her productivity was game-changing. In a culture that equates busy with important, Christina learned to separate her identity from her output. This mindset shift freed her to pursue ideas that genuinely excited her rather than those that simply looked impressive on paper.

Hospitality as Your Secret Weapon

If you've ever worked in hospitality, you might not realize you're sitting on a goldmine of transferable skills. Christina makes a compelling case that hospitality isn't a "soft skill"—it's a leadership superpower that creates real power in any conversation or situation.

Think about what hospitality really means: reading people's needs before they voice them, making others feel comfortable and valued, managing multiple priorities seamlessly, and turning potentially negative experiences into positive ones. These aren't nice-to-have abilities—they're essential leadership competencies that translate directly into business success.

Christina discovered that her hospitality background gave her a unique advantage in difficult conversations and challenging situations. The ability to create connection and find common ground isn't just useful for managing restaurant customers—it's invaluable for negotiating deals, managing team conflicts, and building lasting business relationships.

The Human Connection Advantage

In an increasingly digital world, Christina identified a crucial gap: genuine human connection. Trivia Goat isn't just about asking questions and keeping score—it's about creating spaces where people feel they belong. The technology enables the experience, but the human element drives the value.

What many venue owners misunderstand, according to Christina, is what actually keeps customers coming back. It's not just good food, competitive prices, or convenient location—though those matter. It's the feeling that they're part of something, that they're known and welcomed, that their presence matters to the community.

This insight extends far beyond the hospitality industry. In whatever business you're building, the companies that thrive long-term are those that prioritize human connection alongside their core offering. People don't just buy products or services—they buy belonging, recognition, and the feeling that they matter.

Scaling Without Sacrificing Presence

One of the biggest challenges for any service-based business is scaling without losing the personal touch that made it special in the first place. Christina solved this through what she calls a "self-serve model"—not in the traditional sense, but by packaging her expertise and systems in a way that maintains quality while allowing growth.

The key was recognizing that she didn't need to be physically present at every trivia night to ensure success. By developing robust training systems, clear processes, and empowering her team members, she created a business that could grow without requiring her constant oversight. This approach allowed her to maintain her presence as a mother while building a scalable enterprise.

This is a crucial lesson for any entrepreneur, especially working mothers: scaling doesn't always mean doing more yourself. Sometimes it means stepping back and creating systems that allow others to deliver your vision consistently.

The Moment Everything Clicked

There's a powerful moment Christina describes when she realized she wasn't just running trivia nights—she was creating belonging. Watching strangers become friends, seeing regular customers who planned their weeks around trivia night, witnessing the genuine community that formed around what started as a "silly" business idea—this is when she understood the real value of her work.

This realization is crucial for any entrepreneur questioning whether their idea is "serious enough" or "important enough." Value isn't determined by complexity or prestige—it's determined by how well you solve real human needs. Christina's business succeeds because it addresses a fundamental human need for connection and community in an accessible, enjoyable way.

Permission to Pursue the Unconventional

Perhaps the most important message from Christina's story is the permission to explore unconventional ideas. In a world that often values traditional markers of success, it takes courage to pursue something that others might dismiss as frivolous or unimportant.

Christina's advice is clear: give yourself permission to explore what genuinely interests you, even if it doesn't fit conventional definitions of a "serious business." The market will tell you whether your idea has value—but only if you're brave enough to test it.

Your unconventional idea might just outperform the serious ones because it solves real problems in ways that others overlook. Sometimes the best businesses come from following your natural inclinations and trusting that what brings you joy might also bring value to others.

Christina Wooldridge's journey from hospitality professional to community-building entrepreneur proves that success doesn't require choosing between meaningful work and profitable business. By embracing her natural abilities, leveraging her unique perspective as a working mother, and having the courage to pursue an unconventional idea, she built something that creates both financial success and genuine human connection.

quiz.title: What's Your Connection Catalyst Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally bring people together and build community. Just like Christina Wooldridge turned her gift for connection into a thriving business empire, you have your own unique way of creating belonging.

questions.question: When you walk into a room full of strangers, what's your natural instinct?

options.text: Look for ways to get conversations started—maybe suggest an icebreaker or find common ground to share
Find one or two people to have deeper conversations with rather than working the whole room
Notice who looks uncomfortable and naturally gravitate toward including them
Observe the room dynamics and think about how the space or setup could better serve everyone

questions.question: You have an idea that excites you, but others might think it's 'not serious enough.' How do you typically respond?

options.text: Get even more excited—if it brings joy, it's probably filling a real need people have
Take time to research and validate it quietly before sharing with anyone else
Start talking to people to understand what problem this might actually be solving for them
Begin sketching out how this could work systematically and what it might look like at scale

questions.question: When people describe what you bring to a team or relationship, they usually mention:

options.text: Your ability to make everything more fun and memorable
Your reliability and the way you really listen to what matters to them
Your knack for knowing exactly what someone needs, often before they realize it themselves
Your talent for seeing the bigger picture and helping everyone understand how their part fits

questions.question: You're planning an event or gathering. What aspect do you focus on first?

options.text: What will make this feel special and different from everything else people attend
Making sure every person who comes will feel comfortable and genuinely welcome
Understanding what people really need from this experience, beyond what they're saying they want
Creating a framework that could work for this event and future ones too

questions.question: Your biggest frustration with how most businesses operate is:

options.text: They're so focused on being 'professional' that they forget to be human and enjoyable
They prioritize quick wins over building real, lasting relationships with people
They solve surface problems instead of paying attention to what customers actually need
They make everything more complicated than it needs to be instead of creating simple, effective systems

questions.question: When you think about your ideal work situation, what energizes you most?

options.text: Creating something that brings people joy and gives them stories to tell later
Building something sustainable that will genuinely serve people for years to come
Solving real problems for people, especially ones that others have overlooked or dismissed
Taking your knowledge and expertise and packaging it so more people can benefit from it

results.title: The Experience Creator
results.description: You're the one who turns ordinary moments into memorable experiences. Like Christina organizing childhood parades, you instinctively know how to make people feel special and included. Your superpower lies in seeing possibilities where others see just another Tuesday. This episode will show you how to recognize the business potential in what might feel like 'just being yourself' and help you understand why your seemingly simple ideas could be your most valuable ones.

results.title: The Steady Cultivator
results.description: You build deep, lasting connections through consistency and genuine care. While others chase the spotlight, you're creating the foundation that everything else stands on. Your approach to community-building is methodical and intentional—qualities that become even sharper under pressure. Christina's insights about motherhood as leadership training will resonate deeply with you, showing how your deliberate, thoughtful approach to relationships is actually a rare and valuable business skill.

results.title: The Bridge Builder
results.description: You have a natural gift for seeing what people need and connecting them with solutions—or with each other. You understand that real value comes from relationships, not just transactions. Your ability to spot gaps and fill them is exactly what makes unconventional businesses thrive. This episode will inspire you to trust your instincts about what people are really craving and show you how hospitality skills can become your secret weapon in any industry.

results.title: The Vision Translator
results.description: You excel at taking big ideas and making them accessible, scalable, and real. You see systems where others see chaos, and you know how to package wisdom in ways people can actually use. Like Christina turning her community-building expertise into a tech-enabled business, you understand that scaling doesn't always mean doing more—sometimes it means doing things differently. This episode will validate your approach to growth and show you how unconventional paths often lead to the most meaningful success.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Today on The Manager, I'm joined by a woman who understands something many leaders overlook. The experience is leadership, and connection is built on intention. Christina Wooldridge is a working mom, entrepreneur, and hospitality-driven experience creator. With a background in hotel and restaurant management and nonprofit development, her work has always centered on service, storytelling, and bringing people together.

She's the founder of Trivia Goat, where she leads a team of live hosts creating high-quality trivia experiences that help venues build real community and repeat business. At the heart of everything Christina does is one core belief. When environments are intentional and welcoming, connection follows. And today, we're going to unpack what that looks like in business, leadership, and life. Christina, welcome to The Manager. So happy to have you.

**Christina:** Thank you. I'm super excited. I appreciate the opportunity.

**Aimee:** So before we talk about Trivia Goat, I wanted to start with you. When you look back over your career from hospitality to nonprofits to entrepreneurship, what through line do you see now that maybe you didn't see at the time?

**Christina:** Thank you. I feel like now that I'm in this entrepreneurial side, I've had some time to reflect on how I got here. And so that through line has been pretty apparent in the last couple of years. And it's exciting to find when you can kind of identify it, right? So for me, you touched on it in the intro earlier - hospitality, right? And what that has meant for me is just really service to others in a hospitable way, in a creative way, in a fun way.

I was a hotel restaurant major in college. So that's what I majored in and always loved events, but was still trying to find my footing after college of do I want to work in a hotel? Do I want to work in a restaurant? What is this going to look like? Was always seeking kind of purposeful work. And when I realized that I liked the skill set hospitality provided. I loved serving others, creating memories, creating environments. It kind of led me to fundraising, which was not on my radar. It's not something we chatted about in college. It wasn't a subset of what we were learning, but it had all the same things.

It was about relationship cultivation. It was about great events. It was about creating impressions, being of service to others. But for me, it just had an additional purpose to it. So at the end of the night, when I was shutting down an event, it wasn't just that was fun. It was oh, we raised $200,000 for this really great cause and we get to do it again. So that has always kind of been a part of me. And I've kind of taken that skill set just into different avenues, just into different roles.

I share the story of when I was a child, I played outside a ton and I had a little brother. So he always just had to do what I instructed him to do, right? And creating plays. The big one was parades for me. Always stuffed animal parades on the hour, every hour, because I knew somebody out there was watching. I was always upping the game with my parades. But even from childhood, I can see this idea of moments of fun, moments of environments of creativity were just always who I was and always who I have been.

**Aimee:** That's so fun. It's funny how we can see who we were going to become. Someone told me, just do what you wanted to do when you were four years old and you'll be happy. I think it was Catherine Letson, actually. I forget which episode she is, but it was a fantastic one. And she basically went back to doing what she wanted to do when she was a little kid and she's so happy now.

**Christina:** Yeah. And it may not have been so specific, oh, I want to be a lawyer or a doctor. I think that was kind of the challenging part for me was my road hasn't been specific. It's more of skills that I like to use and things I like to hone in on. But yeah, you look back in childhood and you're like, well, I was planning events on the hour since I was six. So why wouldn't this be something that leads into adulthood?

**Aimee:** Totally. It makes sense to me. I see it in my kids and I'm like, oh, you're bound. Indy is bound to boss something. I get it. I see it in her. There's no way that she's not going to be leading, managing in some way. So you're a working mom running a business built around live experiences. How has motherhood shaped the way you lead, create and think about connection?

**Christina:** Yeah. So live experiences, you know, you get one shot. And it's just you're on, you're on. Motherhood has really, I mean, motherhood's a whirlwind. Let's just put it that way. I mean, it kind of kicked my butt in the beginning because I didn't know how to navigate both or do either well. I was trying to do both at hyper speed levels and that wasn't accomplishing very much. So that in itself has been a journey.

But I would say that in translation to how it's impacted my business and the events that we produce, I've become really hyper-focused on just addressing what needs to be addressed and not getting so involved with all the details, right? You only have a limited amount of time, at least I do, that I can dedicate to work. I try to be as present as possible when my kids are around and the times I've allotted for that. So it's just, it's caused me to just be very intentional about when I'm working. These are the problems we're solving and I'm actually finding myself looking for more long-term solutions versus the quick fixes, right?

Where in the past it might have been okay whatever, let's just cover this hole and move forward, it's I don't want to see this problem again. I don't have time to see this problem again so I need to be really intentional about how we find this solution and we need to kind of package it up and send it off and move on to the next thing. So I think it's just being more mindful of my time and how I'm spending that and being just very focused on the tasks that need to get done and then spending some time on the big picture and then being able to shut it down for a minute and move on and come back to it. But yeah, it's just a lot of intentionality. It's been kind of necessary to kind of accomplish both.

**Aimee:** It's a different season of life. We were talking about it when the camera was off, how different the season of motherhood is, and what a special time it is too, honoring ourselves as mothers and making sure we don't miss these beautiful and special little snowflake moments of motherhood while we're in the pursuit of business. It's great to be in business, but it's how do we balance both?

**Christina:** A hundred percent. And I think most of my life until I became a mother, my value was really centered around my work. And for better or worse, right? So when that's kind of stripped from you, when producing in the same way isn't what your value is equating to, it's a really hard thing to understand and to connect that motherhood is also so valuable and provides so much. But you have to be able to see that and really own it. And it took me a while to get there, honestly.

Because when you're not producing results and numbers and finances or more accounts or whatever, where people can look at you and be like, oh, wow, you're doing a great job. And it's hard to be well, I got my kid to sleep by eight. I mean, that's a feat, right? Nobody cares that I got my kid to sleep at eight. But actually, those things matter. So yeah, for me, I'm only five years in. My daughter's five, my oldest. So I assume there's still tons to learn, which I'm sure I can connect with you on as you get into preteens and all that good stuff.

**Aimee:** Oh, it gets wild though. It just gets different, but as long as you keep your communication with your kids, you just keep your communication going and just never let them push you away. They try to push you away. You squeeze them tighter. Don't ever let them push you away. And never be okay fine I give up on you because that's the point. I was talking to my 16 year old about it yesterday and he said yeah some of his friends have gotten their parents to just kind of give up on them by pushing them away and being so difficult. And I was like I remember when he tried it with me at 14, one of my friends said do not let him push you away. She was like, Aimee, if he tries to push you away, you just squeeze him like a boa constrictor. Do not let him push you away. So I didn't. And it worked. It worked. I got to hang on to him.

**Christina:** Yeah. No, it's great advice. Yeah. No, I'm pocketing that. That was a good one because there was a moment there with him that I thought, oh, he wanted to go stay, we were going to go to Hawaii. And he's like, let me just go stay at my dad's house. And I thought to myself, oh, man, that would be so much easier. And I don't really feel like dealing with him right now. He was being so difficult. And my friend was like, no. She's like, I remember when my daughter did this at this age and I let her go stay with her dad. And it was the worst thing I ever did in my life. She's like, don't do it. I thought at the time I was like, oh, you're being so dramatic. But I think she was 100 percent on point.

**Aimee:** You can see it now.

**Christina:** No, that's big for sure. Yeah. Parenting. It's a whole other thing.

**Aimee:** And so hospitality is often seen as soft work, but it's actually logistics, emotional intelligence, crisis management and leadership. When did you realize that this was real power?

**Christina:** Yeah. So you're absolutely right. It's a lot of working in the moment, especially, live events were kind of my bread and butter. And so something's going to go wrong. You can't really pinpoint it. You don't really know to what degree, but something will go wrong. So pivoting is essential and doing it with a smile on your face and not kind of tainting the user or guest experience is probably your biggest goal, right? And it is a superpower.

I thought it was something anyone could really do, but the more I did it and the more I was around other people who were involved, I was like, oh, they're depending on me to address all that, because everyone's oh, I don't know, they want to step back. So it is a superpower. And I think it's become more relevant, even just in my daily life. Because what I realized is, there's not a lot of people out there who want to serve you. And that can be, I'm shopping around for insurance right now. And it's awful. It's not the most fun phone calls I want to make. And it's very clear that they also don't want to talk to you. You're not a huge priority either.

And so it is a superpower because I recognize that not a lot of people use it. Creating the human connection in any conversation over the phone, in person, it's huge. And it's gotten me really far specifically in fundraising too. You can sit down with someone and just be transactional and say hey, we need this sponsorship. Hey, do you want to give this much? Yes or no? Great. You'll have a table, have fun. We'll see you later. Or you can work on, hey, what is it you're trying to accomplish with your donation? What is it about the org that you really enjoy? What is it that you're looking to change with your financial gift?

And you find out more, you create relationships and now you have this more long-term, robust relationship that you don't have to return to every year hoping that they come out for your event, right? So this hospitable nature can serve in so many different outlets. Can be your business or just trying to get someone on the phone to help you. I've literally stopped conversations when I knew they were going pretty south to say, let's just start over. I just want to connect with you. Is there something I can do to make this easier for you and your job? Is there something that I can provide that would make this conversation turn so that we both can leave here with what we need?

And I do that because, and it'll stop people in their tracks. They'll be like, what? Oh, well, and they soften. I haven't had an encounter where that wasn't something that didn't soften the other person, right? And then you get to navigate things differently. And hopefully, you both win on that one and we hang up satisfied, right? But it's just something that it's not used as often as you think it would be. Even in the service industry, right?

**Aimee:** Yeah. Less and less, right? I feel like less and less, Christina. It's really shocking sometimes.

**Christina:** Yeah. Well, as the generations are going on, there's more technology. There's less actual interpersonal interaction happening. The generation a little younger than you has even less social interaction. So we're kind of losing the real people skills, those how to be hospitable, how to be in the service industry. And as we're losing those, the people who still have them kind of have this hidden secret superpower that we kind of need to be making sure we teach our kids how to talk.

**Aimee:** 100%. And yes, it's maybe considered a soft skill, but it is dying and it's so necessary and just really unfortunate when you have those bad experiences. And those experiences, you have one interaction, it ruins your day and ruins their day. It's just not worth it.

**Christina:** Sometimes I'll leave experiences. And I think back when I was a server or worked in a coffee shop, and by the way, I've had about 1400 jobs. I've been in every job from Sears Portrait Studio to golf cart person to server. I mean, I've been working since I was 14. So I've been in a lot of places and my experience has always been when I'm just nice and of service to others, my day actually goes by faster. It's not even just about them. It's it would help me just get through the day and I'd be oh, already time to clock out, because you're just occupied with doing things.

So I'm always if you only knew, if you just put some more effort, your day would actually be more fun. It's even a benefit for you. So that's always really shocking to me.

**Aimee:** I couldn't agree more. When I worked as a kid in the pharmacy, we had a boutique up front, and if you just could stand there, or you could interact with the customers, and if you talked to the other employees, and you talk to the customers, and you get involved, the day would go so fast. But if no one was there to talk to, the longest days of my life.

**Christina:** Yeah. You can feel the hours, literally. So just a mental note for everyone. Just stay busy, connect, and it'll even make your day better and go faster.

**Aimee:** I love it. I love it. Trivia Goat isn't just about trivia, though. It's community building. What do venue owners misunderstand about what actually keeps people coming back?

**Christina:** Yeah, this is a great question, and I love it because it is about community building. So the way we've approached this business is I really find people who are looking to kind of fill their time with experiences in hosting and they get to go out, host a game, meet people, connect, and then go back to their nine to five, Monday through Friday. And those have been some of the best hosts we have. We've had people who have been with us for over 10 years now because it's just a part of their week. It's just what they look forward to.

Because of that, they've built really strong relationships with the players. So teams will come back week after week. They'll bring their other friends. They might build teams from there. But what keeps them coming back is that relationship with our hosts, right? Credit the specials, the drink specials, the food specials, the promotion on social media absolutely helps. You can't downplay that. But the real bread and butter is that connection that our hosts are making with the players and the teams.

And we have so many locations that that's very evident. It can even be annoying at times because when a host needs a day off, they're sick, they're on vacation. I almost 90% of the time will receive an email from one of the teams that goes consistently saying, where's our host and we don't want this person to come back and our other host gets it and this person doesn't get it. And I'm like, They can have a night off. They can go on vacation for a day, but that's how strong the bond has become where people are I need my person. They make this night for me.

So yeah, for better or worse, those connections can be really strong. And I think when we start with new clients, I really try to express that to them that this is a partnership that yes, we will take care of a lot of the marketing pieces and if you on your end want to do some of the promotions and stuff like that, we'll collaborate. But really it's the partnership that happens at the games. And so our hosts tend to introduce themselves, try to make friends with the staff, because when those bonds are strong, when they know the staff, they know the managers really well, they introduce, they connect with each other.

When people come into that environment, it's a whole different story. The staff is promoting trivia. Trivia is promoting the food, the beverages. It's just a very, very strong partnership that people want to walk into. But when it's too siloed, when it's oh, that's just the company we hire and we're over here. I mean, it can work, but it's not as successful.

**Aimee:** Yeah. So for the people who've never heard of Trivia Goat, just so we don't lose them, could you just pitch it to us real quick so they understand what it is?

**Christina:** Yeah, so Trivia Goat, we specialize in live entertainment. So we do trivia games at bars, breweries, restaurants, where you can utilize tech to submit your answers. So you just use your phone. There's a question appears. You submit your answer. You can see your real-time rankings, your score, and there's prizes at the end. And we just do really fun questions. We try to keep it very, a good variety.

So we're not trying to find the smartest person in the room. We're trying to find the most well-rounded, balanced team. So there's pop culture, there's history, there's science, there's 50-50. If you've gotten no questions right and you just need to pick one, there's options there. We have Name That Tune. So we really just try to build a more well-rounded game. We've been to other games where the questions can be really hard. You get to a point where you're I'm just opting out because I have no idea. We don't want anyone to leave feeling like they didn't contribute. So that's really our goal.

**Aimee:** It sounds like fun.

**Christina:** Yeah, it's fun. And we do themed games throughout the year. We have a lot of Harry Potter requests and a lot of Harry Potter. Taylor Swift has been a really big one this year. So if you think about question answer, we really can customize any game for any topic, including personal topics. We've done birthday parties or retirement parties where it's about the person and stuff. So it's really fun.

**Aimee:** Engagement parties. That'd be so fun. Yeah. The rehearsal dinner.

**Christina:** Yes. Yeah. So that's kind of cool.

**Aimee:** I love it. I love it so much. So you lead a team of live hosts, right? So what's the hardest part of scaling something that relies so heavily on people, presence and personality?

**Christina:** Yeah, I mean, that's a great question. And I would say I'm not even sure I've gotten the whole picture of what that would look like. So managing people for live events is really hard. It's difficult to find the right person. And then once you find the right person who would make a great host, you also have to find the right location. Bars, breweries, some of them are very specific in the type of clients that go there or the vibe they have. And it's not always a fit for every person, right?

So it's about understanding the location vibe, also the new hosts and making that match. So scaling can be challenging. I think for me, I've had to kind of look at the business and say, what is the most important part about this business for me? And to be completely honest, it's the ability to use my skills and things that I'm interested in and building community and creating this awesome team that I love. But also being present as a mom, being present with my family, with my husband, how I can balance that.

So if someone called today and said, I want to give you 20 locations. It's not really something I could jump on right now. I could fill it, but I wouldn't be able to do it well or it would impede on what I'm trying to do with this business. I would be completely thrown in there for a long time, getting 20 locations done on the fly. So we've had to figure out what's scalable and truly our game is great. We love our game. We write great games. And a lot of times people have hosts that can host it in their locations. Their staff knows their clients better than we do. They already work there and they already have those relationships.

So I've actually kind of picked out the pieces that are more scalable in a way that still supports what I'm trying to accomplish, which is more of the balance, right? We have started launching our self-serve option where basically people can utilize our games. They just host it. We train them. We provide tech support. But they're hosting. That allows me to get into anyone's hands, right? Cross states. Because even growing across states with people, that's a whole other layer of HR and what it looks like to have W2 employees in other states and that kind of thing.

So with this model, I'm able to just sell it, have them pick the right person, and then I empower them to go on and have a great game, right? So that's kind of where I land with scaling.

**Aimee:** I like it, Christina. I think that's how you scale. I love that. Yeah. So it may not always be what you're traditionally been doing, it might be taking a look at what works and how to get that into more hands.

**Christina:** Yep. We call that the AC in our family. It's called the adjust and conquer.

**Aimee:** Oh, yes. That is what it is. We see adjust and conquer. Yeah. That's so neat. Sometimes a one little tweak to a business model, because as the world changes, one little tweak can adjust you right into the sweet spot.

I'm like, there it is. What systems or boundaries have you had to put in place to protect your energy while still delivering high touch experiences?

**Christina:** Boundaries are big, right? So at the moment, specifically with Trivia Goat, we went through a season where we didn't have a scheduling procedure for on call or if people dropped out last minute. So if somebody calls oh, I have a flat tire, I can't make it to my location. It's okay, we're scrambling to find someone to fill it and it's really stressful. Because if you don't show up, even one time, it can make or break your relationship with the client, right? They have people sitting there waiting. When is it going to start? When is it going to start? And then nobody shows up. That's a really bad look for them. And it only takes one time for them to say we're done. So it's a big deal.

So I have created some systems now with on-call hosts that kind of now we have backup and we figured out a way to do that. So when that happens, which it happened this morning, it's you just call the on-call person and then they fill in and we figured out how to get everyone the right equipment and we've created location sheets. So all that to be said is, we've had to put some processes in place that work to help us address some of the problems that were reoccurring and were really stressful.

And when it's super stressful, that just bleeds into how I'm at home. And I'm remote, so I can only be on the phone and begging people to pick up shifts, and it's impeding on my mental health. So that's been really good. I also have hired an exceptional general manager. So she's more on the ground there. And she's been sort of my boundary, I suppose, where if hosts have just general questions or they need to reach someone right away, she's usually the first point of contact. Same with clients. If a bar has a question or needs to relay information, she's usually that point person. But from there, she'll give messages to me and when I need to get involved.

And having her has been the best decision we ever made. It's part time for her and she loves it. And she loves being in the field and getting out there and visiting our hosts and creating those relationships. And it's a perfect fit for her. And we love her. But I also don't micromanage her. I mean, we check in, but I don't, have you done this? Hey, have you done that? Hey, I haven't seen this yet. I really have full trust that she's just going to get it done, and if something doesn't happen, we chat about it. But for the most part, I do entrust her to do it well. And that's been huge. It's been huge to get out of the scheduling minutia and sort of that you're the only person anyone can contact for anything. So just things like that have been have been helpful in terms of creating boundaries and and just protecting my space a little bit more.

**Aimee:** I love that. Yeah, it's so nice to get out of that solopreneur kind of world and get into having a little bit more hierarchy within the company. So you have a little bit of a buffer, a little bit of a buffer.

**Christina:** Yeah, totally. It's so nice.

**Aimee:** Was there a moment when you realized you weren't just creating games, you were creating belonging?

And this time I hadn't, we had done a pretty big influx of hiring. So I hadn't personally met a lot of the new hosts. And so I threw, I believe it was a Christmas party, holiday party at the end of the year.

And we actually had a really good turnout. We had 18 people, RSVP, and I don't know, we had a staff of maybe 25. So it was really good. I was oh great, I'm excited. I'm going to meet most of our hosts and this is going to be really great. And so I got there and we just did it at a bar that we partner with. And we had drinks and food and it was really great to see everyone in person.

But I remember stepping back for a second and I noticed that they all kind of knew each other even more, even though they're all at different locations, different nights of the week. **Christina:** So what's a lesson from hospitality or motherhood that you think more founders, especially women, should trust instead of override?

**Aimee:** Yeah. And I think I can speak to the pre-founder, the woman who's kind of on the fence of starting something or thinking, has an idea, right? I think my advice is give yourself permission to explore the uniqueness. My business, it's not traditional, right? If I go to any networking event, I guarantee I'm the only person in the room who has a trivia company. Most people do not. And for a while, I didn't see the value in that. I was actually kind of a little bit hesitant to share because it felt like it wasn't good enough. That it wasn't prestigious enough, that it didn't have enough behind it to share with people this is what I do as a professional.

I limited myself. I limited my relationships. I limited the growth of my company by keeping it here. And so I would just tell people, explore the uniqueness. If you have an idea that you feel isn't really traditional, seems kind of silly, explore it. Think of the life you want to create. And for me, it was flexibility. I need flexibility. I want to be present at these times. How could I do that? Think about what the trickle effect could be. Things that I didn't even know. I didn't know people were going to become friends. I didn't know they're going to support some of our hosts, our musicians, or they're in theater. A lot of them go to their shows and stuff. I didn't know that was going to happen. I couldn't have predicted that.

But by exploring and sharing it, you have no idea what the impact is going to be. And so just give yourself permission to do that. Not all roads are linear. And if you can kind of hone in too on the skill set that aligns with your personality that you've seen since you were maybe even a child, run with that. Don't dismiss it. And I used to dismiss it a lot. I have family members who are lawyers or their road just felt so specific and maybe I should go into the medical industry, or maybe I should look into, well, never law school, but maybe the medical industry, but it wasn't who I am. That's not who I am at all.

And I was dismissing this whole creative side of me that can manage logistics, manage live chaos, who can have creative solutions. I was dismissing all of that because it didn't feel or look or sound something worth investing in, right? So I just would want people to honor who you are, pay attention to what that looks like, and try it. I mean, what's the worst that can happen, right?
